articles.title: The Spirit of Easter Dr. George H. Brimhall
articles.complete_text:
The Spirit of Easter
By Dr. George H. Brimhall

Easter is the greatest of all festivals. It is the festival of festivals. It celebrates the greatest event in all history—the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.

The resurrection is the central doctrine of Christianity. Without it Christianity would be meaningless. Paul said: "If Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain." The resurrection gives meaning to the crucifixion, to the atonement, and to all the teachings of Jesus.

The resurrection is the guarantee of our own resurrection. Because Christ rose from the dead, we too shall rise. Death is not the end. It is but a step in our eternal progress. The grave has no victory. Death has no sting.

The resurrection is the proof of Christ's divinity. It demonstrates that He was indeed the Son of God, with power over death and hell. No mere man could have accomplished what Christ accomplished.

The resurrection is the foundation of our hope. It assures us that righteousness will ultimately triumph over evil, that truth will prevail over error, that light will overcome darkness. It gives us courage to face the trials and tribulations of life, knowing that in the end all will be well.

The spirit of Easter, therefore, is a spirit of joy, of hope, of triumph. It is a spirit that looks beyond the shadows of earth to the glorious realities of eternity. It is a spirit that sees in every ending a new beginning, in every death a resurrection, in every sorrow a joy to come.

May this Easter season bring to each of us a renewed appreciation of the great gift that Christ has given us through His resurrection—the gift of eternal life.
articles.author: Dr. George H. Brimhall

articles.title: Exit Back Yard, Enter Out-door Living Room (Illustrated) Maud Chegwidden
articles.complete_text:
Exit Back Yard, Enter Out-door Living Room
By Maud Chegwidden

THE back yard is passing. In its place comes the out-door living room. No longer is the back yard to be the catch-all for the miscellaneous articles that have no other place. No longer is it to be the unsightly spot that we hope the neighbors will not see. The back yard is being transformed into an attractive, useful out-door living room.

This transformation is the result of a new conception of the possibilities of the space back of the house. We are learning that this space can be made as attractive and as useful as any room in the house. We are learning that it can be a place of beauty, a place of rest and recreation, a place where the family can gather for meals, for games, for quiet enjoyment.

The out-door living room is planned with the same care that is given to the planning of the indoor rooms. It has its floor, its walls, its ceiling, its furniture, its decorations. The floor may be of grass, of gravel, of brick, of concrete, or of flagstone. The walls may be of hedge, of fence, of trellis covered with vines, or of the walls of the house and garage. The ceiling is the sky, but it may be partially covered with an awning, a pergola, or the branches of trees. The furniture includes chairs, tables, benches, and perhaps a hammock or swing. The decorations are the flowers, shrubs, and trees that are planted to make the space beautiful.

The out-door living room serves many purposes. It is a place where meals can be served in pleasant weather. It is a place where the children can play safely. It is a place where the family can gather in the evening for quiet enjoyment. It is a place where friends can be entertained. It is a place where one can work in the garden or pursue a hobby.

The planning of the out-door living room requires careful thought. The space must be divided into areas for different purposes. There should be a dining area, a play area for the children, a quiet area for rest and reading, and perhaps a work area. Each area should be planned to serve its purpose well.

The dining area should be convenient to the kitchen. It should be level and should have a firm surface. It should be partially shaded and should be protected from the wind. A table and chairs suitable for outdoor use are essential.

The play area for the children should be where they can be easily supervised from the house. It should have a smooth, level surface and should be free from hazards. Sand boxes, swings, and other play equipment may be provided.

The quiet area should be in a secluded spot where one can rest and read without interruption. Comfortable chairs and perhaps a hammock should be provided. This area should be shaded and should be planted with flowers and shrubs to make it attractive.

The work area may include space for a vegetable garden, a flower garden, or a workshop. This area should be convenient to the house and should have good drainage.

The out-door living room should be planned as a unit. All parts should be related to each other and should contribute to the beauty and usefulness of the whole. Paths should connect the different areas and should be wide enough for comfortable walking. The planting should be planned to provide privacy, to frame attractive views, and to hide unsightly objects.

The out-door living room is not expensive to create. Much of the work can be done by the family. The materials needed are not costly. The results, however, are very satisfying. The family gains a new room, a room that can be used for many months of the year, a room that adds greatly to the enjoyment of the home.
articles.author: Maud Chegwidden
articles.notes: Complete article found spanning pages 217-220, discussing the transformation of back yards into functional outdoor living spaces

articles.title: Another Victory for the Eighteenth Amendment
articles.complete_text:
Another Victory for the Eighteenth Amendment

Some time ago Federal Judge William Clark of New Jersey startled the country by claiming that the eighteenth amendment had been improperly ratified. We rejoice that the Supreme Court of the United States has vigorously and emphatically upheld the constitutionality of the amendment. We hope that in the future lawmakers everywhere will strive more vigorously to enforce this law and that its beneficial effects will be more and more widely observed.
articles.notes: Found as a short editorial piece on page 229, discussing the Supreme Court's upholding of the Eighteenth Amendment's constitutionality

articles.title: Social Service
articles.complete_text:
Social Service

Lesson 33. The Relief Society and Social Service

Objective: To show the relationship between the Relief Society and social service work.

References: Relief Society Magazine, Vol. XVII, No. 12, pp. 707-715; Vol. XVIII, No. 3, pp. 193-198.

Suggestions for Lesson Development:

1. What is social service work?

2. What relationship does the Relief Society bear to social service work?

3. What are some of the social service activities of the Relief Society?

4. How may Relief Society members prepare themselves for more efficient social service work?

5. What opportunities for social service work exist in your community?

Topics for Discussion:

1. The meaning and scope of social service work.

2. The Relief Society as a social service organization.

3. Specific social service activities of the Relief Society:
   a. Care of the poor and needy
   b. Visiting the sick
   c. Caring for mothers and babies
   d. Work among the aged
   e. Child welfare work
   f. Work with delinquent girls and women
   g. Cooperation with other social agencies

4. Preparation for social service work:
   a. Study and training
   b. Understanding of human nature
   c. Sympathy and tact
   d. Knowledge of community resources

5. Community opportunities for social service.

Questions for Review:

1. Define social service work.

2. In what ways is the Relief Society a social service organization?

3. Name five specific social service activities of the Relief Society.

4. What qualifications should a Relief Society worker have for effective social service work?

5. What social service opportunities exist in your community?
articles.notes: Found the Social Service lesson on page 238. This is part of the lesson department section with structured lesson plans and discussion topics.

articles.title: Explore Your Mind Claire Stewart Boyer
articles.complete_text:
Explore Your Mind
By Claire Stewart Boyer

"The aim of science, art and religion is forever the same—to bring the mind into possession of the truth, goodness and beauty of the world." The aim of science is to find a universe that works, the aim of art and religion is to find a universe that is significant." This statement which opens Mr. Wiggam's great book gives the scope of a mind that can see world problems in their true relationship and can state them, fearlessly and with a satisfying certainty. Mr. Wiggam is just as efficient in dealing with individual problems. He tells us that Character tests have proved that 80 per cent of the failures in life are due to things which the individual can control.

In addition to the physical, mental and emotional phases of personality he adds social intelligence and will power. Social intelligence is the ability to judge, appreciate and adjust. The person who can judge himself well, usually overestimates others. He is interested in others, makes human contacts easily and successfully, stirs others to enthusiasms. He is the inspirational type, the leader. He who judges others well, is the cold-blooded driver. He is interested most in himself. He should choose and hire employees but never direct them. He is the egoist. Overestimation of self reveals stupidity, good estimation of self reveals intelligence.

Desirable qualities in men seem to be linked together. The able person has common sense, sociability, talent and appreciation. "Rate yourself, compare yourself, with others," says Wiggam.

Success depends greatly upon will power. No one is denied a measure of success who will use this great force. Recreate your lost enthusiasms, overcome fear, develop the qualities you admire through using will power. One woman learned to use a paralyzed arm through will power. Fear shuts up the pathway of expression and response. It binds one to failure. Will power can overthrow fear. Don't let your feelings overcome you. Action is the remedy for the blues.

Only the self controlled person is free. Mental and emotional tests are now given for an estimate of the worth of an individual. If he has established definite, purposeful habits, coordinated and controlled them he will be an efficient human being. Imagination, reason and memory are merely habit responses. If the memory is weak, the habit has not been well developed, has been mistreated or ignored. It is startling to know that the genius and the idiot think in the same way, only the higher mind makes better use of thought with thought in order to be able to retain and recall them, to build and create with them.

Few people even begin to live up to their possibilities. The object of vocational guidance is to make accomplishment run parallel to ability. To make the most of life, physical, mental, social, emotional and will habits should be carefully developed.

The establishment of these habits is education, and education is life.
articles.author: Claire Stewart Boyer
articles.notes: Found complete article on page discussing mind exploration and self-improvement based on Mr. Wiggam's work
